title: immortality through children
wikitext:
[]: immortality through children             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Oct 23, 2013 6:58 pm Stillborn wrote: Resignation Because the tragedy is, that you will die in a farce anyway and unless you are someone exceptional, you are really powerless to change it and can only resign yourself to unavoidable.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Oct 23, 2013 7:21 pm Dataprime wrote: So, Evangelion is really just a love story?

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Oct 23, 2013 7:44 pm Chuckman wrote: It kid of is. One of the major theme is the acceptance of the grief that comes with the joy of being with other people. Like knowing with absolute certainty that your mom and other vital people who are older than you will die and there is nothing you can do to stop it.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Oct 23, 2013 7:50 pm I don't think everybody who comes back from the LCL is immortal. I didn't mean knowing they will die immediately in a plot sense, I meant recognizing the inevitability of death.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Oct 23, 2013 8:11 pm Chuckman wrote: I don't think everybody who comes back from the LCL is immortal. I didn't mean knowing they will die immediately in a plot sense, I meant recognizing the inevitability of death.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Oct 23, 2013 8:21 pm Bagheera wrote: I can't see how that's addressed in NGE/EoE at all. It doesn't seem to enter the story at any point

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Oct 23, 2013 8:30 pm Chuckman wrote: Shinji's whole relationship with his mother revolves around the fact that children typically outlast their parents. Part of the reason he has to overcome and escape his dependence on her is because she can't shelter him forever.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Oct 23, 2013 8:38 pm I'm talking about themes and symbolism here, not giant robot mechanics. Yes, Yui is literally immortal. It's still making a statement about the natural impermanence of the parent-child relationship and the need to grow beyond it to prosper as a mature person. Yui's immortality as a giant petrified killing machine is relevant to the plot but not especially relevant to the theme. Are you seriously arguing that Eva is sorta about love? Every single character is shaped by love (either romantic or familial or both) and the way love (or the lack of it) helps and hurts a person. The Evas are powered by love.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Oct 23, 2013 8:44 pm Chuckman wrote: I'm talking about themes and symbolism here, not giant robot mechanics. Yes, Yui is literally immortal. It's still making a statement about the natural impermanence of the parent-child relationship and the need to grow beyond it to prosper as a mature person. Yui's immortality as a giant petrified killing machine is relevant to the plot but not especially relevant to the theme.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Oct 23, 2013 8:54 pm Bagheera wrote: And my point is that looking at the plot is kinda important when determining just what she show's themes actually are.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Wed Oct 23, 2013 9:09 pm I can't really say I've ever perceived this "btw your parents are gonna kick it" theme in Eva either but regardless of that if you want to continue this tangent make new topic for it.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Oct 23, 2013 9:11 pm Chuckman wrote: The fact that she, technically, has survived this, is irrelevant to theme of accepting the death of loved ones and moving on.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Oct 25, 2013 6:39 pm Decided to give this a second chance at life.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Oct 25, 2013 6:58 pm Bagheera wrote: It would be if said theme showed up anywhere else in the show, but it doesn't.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Oct 25, 2013 7:18 pm For what it's worth that theme CAN be read elsewhere in the show though showing us the negative consequences of not doing so. Misato's need for vengence out of survivor's guilt, Asuka's inferiority complex caused by being unable to let go of her mother's criticisms (while impaired), maybe even some shadow of Naoko still lingering over Ritsuko's accomplishments and giving her a reason to ignore questions of ethics along the way. This is going into fanfic zone, but It's not impossible to speculate that Gendo's clinginess even beyond 'death', his jealousy of Shinji taking Yui's attentions from him, and the degree to which he was clearly whipped have their roots in similar longing for an absent mother figure. Say, perhaps his own mother died in childbirth and his father blamed him for it? Would explain a hell of a lot about his being used to others disliking him, his need for control, his inability to be an effective father for fear that he'd just fuck it all up, and his clear disdain for authority figures while being able to suck it up and manipulate them. In a way the whole moving past the death of others idea is just a subset of the larger theme of learning to accept yourself on your own terms, as well as the theme of generational conflicts.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Oct 25, 2013 7:27 pm I think there's a lot to be said for a fanfic exploring such themes, but I still consider it a stretch to claim they are somehow main themes of the show. IOW DOA can make them plausible and valid, but they are not by any stretch the default reading of the show, nor, IMO, are they intended to be such.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Oct 25, 2013 8:10 pm NemZ wrote: his jealousy of Shinji taking Yui's attentions from him
summary:
[]: immortality through children             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Oct 23, 2013 6:58 pm Stillborn wrote: Resignation Because the tragedy is, that you will die in a farce anyway and unless you are someone exceptional, you are really powerless to change it and can only resign yourself to unavoidable.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Oct 23, 2013 7:21 pm Dataprime wrot
